title: Two People Who Never Agree
excerpt: My parents are looking at buying a second place. My dad loved it. My mom doesn't want it. They won't agree. They rarely do.
content:
What happened

My parents are looking at buying a second place.

They saw a house this weekend that my dad loved. It checked everything for him. Space, light, a place he could settle into.

My mom doesn't want it. She wants a small condo. Something contained. Something that doesn't feel like a full shift.

They won't agree. They rarely do.

This isn't new. It's the same pattern I've seen my whole life.

---

What I'm telling myself

They're just different people.

My dad is structured, principled, internal. He values order, reverence, consistency.

My mom is emotional, outward, high energy. She wants community, expression, movement.

Even with something like church, they couldn't align. He wanted something formal and traditional. She wanted something alive and expressive. They stopped going altogether because they couldn't agree.

So this is just another version of that.

---

What's probably actually true

They don't just "see things differently." They avoid resolving the difference.

That's the pattern.

Instead of forcing alignment or making a clear tradeoff, things stall. Nothing moves unless it works for both of them, and almost nothing does.

So decisions just… don't happen.

This house is the same thing. It's not really about the house.

My dad is ready to move toward something new.

My mom is trying to hold onto what she already has.

And neither of them is going to fully give.

---

What I'm noticing about my mom

I think she's scared.

Not in an obvious way. But in a structural way.

She doesn't like change. She doesn't travel much. She relies a lot on her immediate environment and her people.

Charlotte is stable. It's familiar. It's where her friends are.

A second home isn't just a purchase. It's a signal that things are shifting.

And I don't think she wants that.

So the condo isn't really about preference. It's about control. Keeping one foot in the life she knows.

---

What I'm noticing about my dad

He's more willing to move forward, but only on his terms.

He knows what he wants. He has a clear picture. And he doesn't bend easily.

There's a kind of pride in that. Also a kind of rigidity.

If it doesn't match his standard, it's not worth doing.

So you end up with two people who won't fully give, just in different ways.

---

What I'm feeling but not saying

I don't know what to make of this.

On one hand, they've been married for a long time. There's something real there. They've made it work.

On the other hand, it doesn't feel like alignment. It feels like coexistence with friction.

And I don't know if that's what I want.

There's also something uncomfortable in how much I see myself in my dad.

The internal nature. The sense of principle. The unwillingness to compromise on certain things.

I don't think I'm as rigid as him. But I'm probably closer to him than I want to admit.

And I don't know what that means for how I show up in relationships.

---

Open questions

Is being with someone very different actually a strength, or just a constant source of tension?

At what point does "we see things differently" become avoidance of hard decisions?

Am I going to default to holding my ground like my dad, even when it costs progress?

Do I actually want alignment, or do I just assume relationships are supposed to work like this?
